metrics.narrative: No quantified business problem, savings target, or cost-of-inaction has been captured for Sasol. There are no Avoma calls to extract buyer-stated pain, and the synthesis-map metric fields returned no data. The only signal is the October 2025 Next_Step note that Sasol is in the planning stage for a project, which implies a future-state initiative rather than an acute, quantified pain. The product scope (ML iAnalyze, iSave, iSupplier, iRisk) points at spend visibility, savings identification, supplier and risk management, but the buyer has never confirmed these are their priorities or attached a number to them. The move is a discovery or re-engagement call to surface and quantify the operational or financial problem, the cost of delay, and the expected ROI.

sources: SF Next_Step__c (March 2026 workshop offer not taken up)
champion.narrative: No champion has been identified or developed. Five operational contacts are on the opportunity (Hendrik Steynberg, VP Inbound Supply Chain Management; Deon Meyer, Manager: the deal owner's manager / Project Portfolio Manager; Jacobus de Wet, Lead Sourcing: Facilities and Civil (Acting); Estee Larkins, Snr Commercial Contract Specialist at Sasol; Thabiso Seboka, Improvement Specialist), but none is designated Champion and none has shown advocacy, introduced Zycus to power, or driven internal momentum. The March 2026 Next_Step states the RFI stalled despite the offered design workshop, which is a strong signal that no internal sponsor is pushing on Zycus's behalf, and the 170-day silence reinforces it. The move is to re-engage Hendrik Steynberg or Deon Meyer, test their willingness to advocate, and if neither will, recruit a different internal sponsor.

competition.narrative: The competitive landscape is unknown. Competitors__c is empty, the free-text competition fields returned no data, and there are no calls to reveal rivals or the incumbent stack. The March 2026 RFI reference implies a possible multi-vendor evaluation, but no vendor is named and it is equally possible the initiative is early and sole-sourced against the status quo. For a large procurement transformation the real competition may simply be Sasol's current manual or ERP-native reporting and internal inertia, which the stall would be consistent with. The move is a direct discovery call to map the competitive set, understand the current procurement and analytics stack, and confirm whether the RFI is multi-vendor.

identify_pain.narrative: The specific operational or financial pain Sasol is trying to solve has not been articulated. The only signal is the October 2025 planning-stage reference, which describes intent rather than an acute problem, and there is no call evidence of the buyer describing manual-process burden, savings leakage, supplier risk, or compliance exposure. The product scope suggests those pain areas but the buyer has not confirmed them, and no pain owner has been named. The move is a structured pain-discovery session with the operational leads (Hendrik Steynberg, Deon Meyer) to surface the problem, name the pain owner, and quantify the cost of inaction.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec approval, and implementation scoping are all unknown. No MSA, SOW, or NDA activity is logged and no calls exist to reveal the paper path. Given Sasol's size and South African base, cross-border data residency, POPIA compliance, and likely SAP ERP integration are probable contract and implementation topics, but none have been scoped. The October 2025 Next_Step mentioned attacking through EY and inviting Sasol to a roundtable, but no EY engagement or scoping has been logged. The move is to qualify the procurement and legal path and confirm whether EY has any role, once a live buyer conversation is re-established.

sources: SF OpportunityContactRole (5 contacts, none EB/Exec Sponsor)
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer is identified or engaged. None of the five OpportunityContactRole contacts carries an Economic Buyer or Executive Sponsor designation, and the synthesis-map budget fields returned no data. The most senior mapped contact is Hendrik Steynberg, VP Inbound Supply Chain Management, who is a plausible operational sponsor but not a confirmed budget owner, and he has not been engaged since January 2026. With no call history there is no evidence of who controls commercial or pricing authority at Sasol. Identifying and reaching the budget owner (likely at the supply-chain or finance VP level, above the operational leads) is a ranked move.

decision_process.narrative: The buying and approval process Sasol is running is not documented. The stage history shows churn (Qualified to the relevant stakeholder on 30 Mar 2026 and back to Qualified on 01 Jul 2026) driven by the rep, not by observed buyer milestones. An RFI was referenced in March 2026 but no procurement owner, evaluation timeline, or approval chain has been confirmed, and there are no calls to read the process from a meeting sequence. As a large South African chemicals and energy enterprise, Sasol will almost certainly run a formal, multi-stakeholder procurement, but the specifics are unmapped. The move is to ask a mapped contact to walk through the evaluation stages and approval steps.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria Sasol would score vendors against are unknown. The March 2026 Next_Step references an RFI, which normally encodes formal requirements, but the RFI content was never captured and the note says it gained no traction. No use-case reviews, test cases, or workshop feedback exist because no calls were held. Without the RFI requirements or any discovery, we cannot say what Sasol values (spend analytics depth, supplier risk, AI/automation, ERP fit). The move is to obtain the RFI or run a requirements-discovery session so the criteria can be documented and mapped to the Zycus differentiators.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.
blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.text: Buyer is leaning elsewhere — No buyer calls in the last 12 months; no Zycus-specific preference or comparison expressed.

blocks.title: What moves it forward

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 17 is driven by Verdict 'Off Track' — weak forward intent. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.win_position: Win 5 is carried by momentum 54 vs stage-expected 50. Offsetting it: preference strength -0.50 (weight 20).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 54 (roughly flat): Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Partial Read: 4 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, stage integrity, exec and champion, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 0 is anchored on the stage win (5), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 54, and dragged by risk 17. Read is Partial Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

contributions.evidence: Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

deal_movement.summary: Movement over the last year is rep-side administration, not buyer progression. The close date has been pushed repeatedly (2026-04-22 to 2026-03-31 in Aug 2025, to 2026-06-30 in Dec 2025, and out to 2027-02-26 in Mar 2026), ownership changed to Caroline Lacocque in Dec 2025, and the stage bounced Qualified to Initial Interest and back to Qualified on 01 Jul 2026. The last real buyer touch was 2026-01-15; everything since is internal.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is dark and cooling further; the freshest genuine buyer signal is the May 2026 Gartner follow-up interest, which has not converted to any logged activity.

north_star_verdict.math: Close date 2027-02-26 is 238 days out from today (2026-07-03). Created 2025-07-14, so this opportunity is roughly 355 days old and only reached Qualified on 2026-07-01 after bouncing Qualified to Initial Interest (30 Mar 2026) and back. Last verified buyer activity was 2026-01-15, about 170 days ago, which forces a dark read. The seven 2026-07-01 tasks are rep-side Call Attempted health nudges, not buyer engagement. Required pace to close cannot be assessed while the buyer is unresponsive; re-engagement, not pace, is the gating issue.
evidence: LastActivityDate 2026-01-15, about 170 days ago, forces a dark state (SF LastActivityDate)

north_star_verdict.headline: Deal has been dark since 15 Jan 2026 (roughly 170 days with no buyer touch); the RFI stalled, the offered design workshop was never taken up, and the EY partner-led motion has produced no logged progress.

flags: Re-open the relationship first: the owner must land one direct call with Hendrik Steynberg or Deon Meyer before any workshop or commercial step, because the deal has had no buyer touch since 15 Jan 2026.
Map power early: no Economic Buyer or Decision Maker is designated across five operational contacts, so the next live call must surface the budget owner and the RFI decision process.

Convert the Gartner interest into a working session: the May 2026 follow-up interest is the freshest positive signal, so anchor re-engagement to a specific value-led design workshop with a proposed date.
best_practice_check.summary: This is an early-stage, dark deal; the win-strategy priority is to manufacture a live buyer conversation and use it to map power, pain, and the competitive set before any commercial motion.

customer_preference.evidence: No buyer calls in the last 12 months; no Zycus-specific preference or comparison expressed.

competitive_position.summary: The competitive field is genuinely unknown, not sole-sourced by choice. Competitors__c is empty, no Avoma calls exist to name rivals or the incumbent, and the Next_Step notes reference an RFI (March 2026) without naming vendors. The most likely current competitor is the status quo (Sasol's existing manual or ERP-native procurement reporting) plus internal inertia, which fits the RFI stall and the 170-day silence. No named vendor threat can be asserted on this evidence; mapping the competitive set is a discovery task, not a settled read.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: The Merlin / AI story has never been put in front of Sasol; with zero calls there is no evidence the agentic narrative has landed or been rejected, so positioning is effectively absent rather than weak.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: No expressed buyer criteria exist to align against because there are no calls and the RFI content was never captured. Product-scope intent (spend analytics, savings, supplier and risk management, AI) is inferred, not confirmed by Sasol.
